does: This paper adapts mechanistic interpretability techniques from NLP to transformer-based time series classifiers, applying activation patching, attention saliency analysis, and sparse autoencoders to uncover internal causal mechanisms. The authors systematically probe individual attention heads and timesteps within these models, constructing causal graphs that trace information flow during classification tasks. They use sparse autoencoders to identify interpretable latent features and demonstrate which specific attention heads and temporal positions drive correct predictions on benchmark time series datasets, moving beyond standard input-output attribution methods.
connects: This work extends the mechanistic interpretability literature, which has primarily focused on large language models and vision transformers, to the time series domain. It builds on foundational techniques like activation patching developed for understanding causal mechanisms in NLP, attention analysis methods that probe head-level contributions, and sparse autoencoders for discovering interpretable representations. The paper responds to the broader challenge of understanding transformer opacity that has motivated recent work in the interpretability community, while addressing the specific underexplored problem of explaining transformer decision-making in temporal classification tasks.
enables: This work opens pathways for building more transparent time series classification systems and identifying failure modes through interpretable representations. Understanding the causal mechanisms in temporal transformers could enable more robust model debugging, architectural improvements informed by mechanistic insights, and development of sparse, efficient transformers tailored to time series. The interpretability framework developed here could facilitate applications in safety-critical domains like medical monitoring or anomaly detection where understanding classifier reasoning is essential, and it sets a template for applying NLP-derived mechanistic methods to other specialized transformer architectures.
